method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The island is identified in the sources by the Japanese name Nishinoshima and is described as part of the Ogasawara island chain south of Tokyo.[2] Following the 1973 eruption, the Maritime Safety Agency named the newly emerged landform Nishinoshima Shintō on 21 December 1973, distinguishing it from the pre-existing island.[3] The name Nishinoshima Shintō was applied after the eruption produced new land above sea level in September 1973, which later became connected with the older island by volcanic deposits.[3]

==Geology==
Nishinoshima is an andesitic volcanic island at the summit of a large submarine volcano approximately 30 km in diameter.[3] Before its recent enlargement, the island was described as about 650 m long from southwest to northeast, about 200 m wide, 77,000 m² in area, and 25 m high, with several reefs on its northeastern side.[3]

The first recorded eruption began in May 1973 on the seafloor about 400 m east of the southern tip of the island, and by September it had formed a new island.[3] The 1973 eruption began as an undersea phreatomagmatic explosion, later producing volcanic blocks and lava flows from five craters.[3] Although much of the new volcanic edifices was removed by marine erosion, reworked deposits eventually linked the old and new landmasses, and sediment movement closed the mouth of the intervening bay by 1982.[3] The silica content of ejecta from this activity ranged from 51.8 to 60.1 wt %, consistent with basaltic andesite to andesite compositions.[3]

Nishinoshima has remained volcanically active in the modern period, with eruptive activity from 2013 to 2020 divided into four episodes.[4] The 2013–2015 episode lasted about two years, while later episodes in 2017 and 2018 were shorter and had lower eruptive volumes, before activity intensified again during the 2019–2020 episode.[4] Average effusion rates were estimated at about 2.0 × 10⁵ m³ per day for the 2013–2015 eruption and 1.5 × 10⁵ m³ per day for the 2017 eruption.[4]

Geochemical analysis of submarine deposits from the 2020 eruption indicates that Nishinoshima erupted basalt, basaltic andesite, andesite, and dacite magmas during that event.[4] The andesites from 2020 are similar to those erupted from the island since 1973, while the 2020 basalts resemble older basalts known from surrounding submarine knolls.[4] These findings suggest that basaltic magma may have been generated throughout Nishinoshima’s history, but that its eruptive center shifted to the island summit in 2020.[4] Nishinoshima andesitic primary magmas are interpreted to originate directly from the mantle through shallow, hydrous melting of plagioclase peridotite.[4]

==Ecology==
Repeated eruptive disturbance has made Nishinoshima a natural field site for studying primary succession, species colonization and ecosystem development on newly formed volcanic land.[2][5] Monitoring has focused on the arrival and establishment of organisms on the island, as well as the development of surrounding marine habitats such as coral reefs.[5]  

The island’s terrestrial flora is highly vulnerable to renewed volcanic activity, which can remove much of the existing plant cover.[2] Before the 2019 eruption, researchers from Tokyo Metropolitan University collected several plants of the island’s purslane population, identified as *Portulaca oleracea*, and the plant was later reported extinct on Nishinoshima.[2] Although *P. oleracea* occurs in other tropical regions, its local disappearance from Nishinoshima illustrates how small island populations can be eliminated by volcanic disturbance even when the species is not globally rare.[2]  

As part of the Ogasawara Islands, Nishinoshima is relevant to studies of evolution on isolated oceanic islands, a region noted for high endemism and biological divergence from mainland Asia.[5] Ongoing research treats the island as a living laboratory for observing colonization, ecological succession, marine ecosystem formation and resilience after repeated disturbance.[5]
